items.q: What is an adjacency pair in conversation analysis?
items.correct: A two-part sequence where the first part conditions the second — question/answer, greeting/greeting, request/acceptance-or-refusal. The second part is 'conditionally relevant' on the first.
items.misconception: Adjacency pairs are two people who sit next to each other while talking.
items.random_wrong: Adjacency pairs are pairs of synonyms in vocabulary lists.
items.why: Adjacency pairs reveal that turns aren't independent — a question expects an answer, and silence after one is heard as meaningful. Students take 'adjacency' too literally (physical proximity) rather than structural sequencing.

items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.q: What is the telegraphic stage of child language development?
items.correct: Around age 2-3, children produce short utterances containing content words but omitting function words and inflections (e.g. 'mummy gone shop').
items.misconception: It is the stage when children first start using a telephone.
items.random_wrong: It is the stage where children memorise long stories.
items.why: The name comes from telegrams — which dropped articles and prepositions to save money. Students misread the term literally rather than understanding the analogy.

items.q: The IRF structure was proposed by:
items.why: It's Sinclair & Coulthard. “Sacks, Schegloff & Jefferson” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: A point where turn change is legitimate
items.why: It's A point where turn change is legitimate. “A pause for breath only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The Sacks, Schegloff and Jefferson turn-taking paper appeared in:
items.why: The real figure is 1974. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
